title: ResNet Variants: Basic Blocks and Bottlenecks
concept: Inside the ResNet family, shallower models like ResNet-18 and ResNet-34 use simple basic blocks, while deeper models switch to bottleneck blocks that use 1x1 convolutions to squeeze and expand channels so depth stays practical.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What makes every model in the ResNet family a ResNet?

options.text: It is built from residual blocks with skip connections
It replaces all convolutions with pooling
It only works if every layer is 1x1

exercises.explain: Right — the family resemblance comes from residual wiring first. The variants change what happens inside those residual blocks.

cards.title: Same family, different block bodies
cards.body: At a glance, ResNet-18, ResNet-34, and ResNet-50 all sound like bigger or smaller copies of one idea. They are related — but not identical twins. The deeper members of the family change the internal block design, not just the layer count.

cards.title: Basic block vs. bottleneck block
cards.body: A basic block usually uses two 3x3 convolutions on the main path. A bottleneck block wraps the expensive 3x3 between two 1x1 convolutions: one to squeeze channels down, one to expand them back up. Both are still residual blocks because both add their learned path back to a shortcut.

cards.title: One is a simple hinge, one is a folding tool
cards.body: A basic block is like a plain two-hinge door: sturdy and straightforward. A bottleneck block is like a folding travel bike: it compresses itself before the costly part, then opens back up at the end. Same job category, smarter packaging for longer journeys.

cards.title: How the family splits
cards.body: ResNet-18 and ResNet-34 are the shallower siblings, so their residual blocks can stay simple: 3x3 -> 3x3. ResNet-50 and deeper variants need many more layers, so they usually switch to 1x1 -> 3x3 -> 1x1 bottleneck blocks to keep the stack affordable.

cards.title: Deeper variants are not just 'more of the same'
cards.body: A common myth is that ResNet-50 is literally ResNet-34 plus extra copied blocks. Not quite. The deeper variants still use skip connections, but the inside of the residual block changes. That block redesign is a major reason the deeper family members make practical sense.

cards.title: Two residual block styles
cards.body: The shortcut idea stays; the internal path changes.

left.text:
Shortcut + main path
3x3 -> 3x3
Used in shallower ResNets

right.text:
Shortcut + main path
1x1 -> 3x3 -> 1x1
Used in deeper ResNets

exercises.prompt: Which statement best describes the architectural split inside the classic ResNet family?

options.text: Shallower models often use basic blocks, while deeper models often use bottleneck blocks
Every ResNet uses exactly the same block shape regardless of depth
Only bottleneck blocks are residual; basic blocks are not
Basic blocks remove skip connections so they can stay simple

exercises.explain: Exactly — the family keeps the residual idea but changes block style as depth grows.

exercises.prompt: True or false: a bottleneck block stops being a residual block just because it contains 1x1 convolutions.
exercises.explain: False — it is still residual because the learned path is still added to a shortcut path.

exercises.sentence: Basic ResNet blocks are commonly built from two ___ convolutions on the main path.
exercises.explain: Right — the classic basic block is the straightforward two-3x3 version.

exercises.prompt: Which statement sounds most like a real explanation of ResNet variants, not a shallow catalog?

options.text: The family keeps residual shortcuts fixed while swapping between simple and bottleneck-style block interiors depending on how deep the model gets
The model number is just decoration and tells you nothing about block design
All variants are identical except for the font in the paper title

exercises.explain: Yes — the intuitive view is 'same residual family, different block bodies.'

exercises.prompt: A learner says, 'If ResNet-50 is deeper, why not just keep stacking the same simple 3x3 basic blocks forever?' What is the best answer?

options.text: Because deeper ResNets usually switch to bottleneck blocks, which keep the same residual idea but make the expensive spatial work cheaper enough to scale depth
Because skip connections only work when there are exactly 18 layers
Because basic blocks cannot contain any convolution at all
Because ResNet-50 is actually shallower than ResNet-34 once you ignore the name

exercises.explain: Exactly. The family did not just pile on more depth blindly. It changed the block design from simple basic blocks to bottleneck blocks so the residual strategy could scale. Next, we'll zoom in on that bottleneck pattern itself.
